abstract.para: To perform maintenance tasks on the cluster nodes, you might need to stop the resources running on that node, to move them, or to shut down or reboot the node. It might also be necessary to temporarily take over the control of resources from the cluster, or even to stop the cluster service while resources remain running. This chapter explains how to manually take down a cluster node without negative side-effects. It also gives an overview of different options the cluster stack provides for executing maintenance tasks.
When you shut down or reboot a cluster node (or stop the cluster services on a node), the following processes are triggered:

listitem.para: The resources that are running on the node are stopped or moved off the node.
If stopping a resource fails or times out, the &stonith; mechanism fences the node and shuts it down.

section.title: General workflow
section.para: If you need to do testing or maintenance work, follow the general steps below.
Otherwise, you risk unwanted side effects, like resources not starting in an orderly fashion, unsynchronized CIBs across the cluster nodes, or even data loss.

step.para: Before you start, choose the appropriate option
Execute your maintenance task or tests.
After you have finished, put the resource, node or cluster back to normal operation.

section.title: Options
section.para: &pace; offers the following options for performing system maintenance:

varlistentry.term: Putting the cluster in maintenance mode

listitem.para: The global cluster property maintenance-mode puts all resources into maintenance state at once. The cluster stops monitoring them and becomes oblivious to their status. Only the resource management by &pace; is disabled. &corosync; and SBD are still functional. Use maintenance mode for any tasks involving cluster resources. For any tasks involving infrastructure, such as storage or networking, the safest method is to stop the cluster services completely.

varlistentry.term: Stopping the cluster services for the whole cluster

listitem.para: Stopping the cluster services on all nodes at once allows you to shut down a cluster while avoiding the mass migration of resources that would happen if you shut down each node one by one. Because there are no nodes to migrate to, all resources are stopped.

varlistentry.term: Putting a node in maintenance mode

listitem.para: This option allows you to put all resources running on a specific node into maintenance state at once. The cluster ceases monitoring them and becomes oblivious to their status.

varlistentry.term: Putting a node in standby mode

listitem.para: A node that is in standby mode can no longer run resources. Any resources running on the node are moved away or stopped (if no other node is eligible to run the resource). Also, all monitoring operations are stopped on the node (except for those with role="Stopped"). You can use this option if you need to stop a node in a cluster while continuing to provide the services running on another node.

varlistentry.term: Stopping the cluster services on a node

listitem.para: This option stops all the cluster services on a single node. Any resources running on the node are moved away or stopped (if no other node is eligible to run the resource). This is risky because if stopping a resource fails or times out, the node is fenced. Therefore, we recommend using standby or maintenance mode before stopping the cluster services on a node.

varlistentry.term: Putting a resource in maintenance mode

listitem.para: When this mode is enabled for a resource, no monitoring operations are triggered for the resource. Use this option if you need to manually touch the service that is managed by this resource and do not want the cluster to run any monitoring operations for the resource during that time.

varlistentry.term: Putting a resource into unmanaged mode

listitem.para: The is-managed meta attribute allows you to temporarily release a resource from being managed by the cluster stack. This means you can manually touch the service that is managed by this resource (for example, to adjust any components). However, the cluster continues to monitor the resource and to report any failures. If you want the cluster to also cease monitoring the resource, use the per-resource maintenance mode instead.
